title: The road before the Strip
body: Las Vegas Boulevard was once Highway 91, the main road into town from Southern California. North of Sahara, restored vintage signs form a neon trail toward downtown.

title: Named at a nearly empty intersection
body: In the 1930s, only two families lived near what is now Decatur and Vegas Drive. Resident Leonard Frechette chose the road's name when the other family declined.

title: UNLV's dirt-road beginning
body: UNLV opened beside Maryland Parkway in 1957, when the road was still two lanes of dirt. The original campus occupied just 80 acres.

title: Follow it out of town
body: Drive west on Charleston and the city gives way to State Route 159 and Red Rock Canyon. The conservation area lies about 17 miles west of the Strip on the same corridor.

title: Where the city stops
body: At the Strip, Sahara Avenue marks the boundary of the City of Las Vegas. Most of the famous resort corridor to its south is actually in unincorporated Clark County.

title: The casino is gone; the road remains
body: Tropicana Avenue was named for the resort at Las Vegas Boulevard. The Tropicana was demolished in 2024, but its name remains on one of the valley's major east-west roads.

title: The road along Sunset Park
body: Sunset forms the park's northern edge; Warm Springs forms its southern edge, and Eastern borders it on the west. Together they make a useful three-road landmark beside the airport.

title: Three names, one crooked corridor
body: Spring Mountain runs through Chinatown to Las Vegas Boulevard. Across the Strip it becomes Sands Avenue, then bends south and joins Twain Avenue.

title: Half of an airport pair
body: Near the airport, Paradise carries traffic south while University Center Drive, formerly Swenson Street, serves as its northbound counterpart. Together they form a split one-way corridor.

title: Before the freeway, this was the connection
body: Boulder Highway was once the only road linking downtown Las Vegas, Henderson, and Boulder City. Motels, casinos, and roadside businesses grew along the route before US 95 drew traffic away.

title: Stephanie is a real person
body: Stephanie Street was named for Stephanie Page Wurzer when she was a child and the route was still an isolated dirt road. It later became one of Henderson's major commercial streets.

title: Not Lake Mead Boulevard
body: Henderson's Lake Mead Parkway is different from Lake Mead Boulevard in the north valley. Older signs and businesses may still call it Lake Mead Drive, its name until around 2004 or 2005.

title: Two big parks on one line
body: Buffalo passes All American Park near Charleston and Centennial Hills Park in the northwest. Those two large green spaces make useful anchors for opposite ends of the road.

title: A road full of park markers
body: Durango passes Angel Park near Charleston, Durango Hills Park farther north, and Thunderbird Family Sports Complex near Ann. The park names help track the road up the west valley.

title: Look for the five ballfields
body: Rainbow Family Park sits just off Rainbow at Oakey Boulevard. Its five baseball fields and 26 acres make it a recognizable midpoint on this long west-valley road.

title: The Blue Angel watches Five Points
body: At Eastern, Charleston, and Fremont stands the restored 15-foot Blue Angel from a 1957 motel. The statue was designed by Betty Willis, who also created the Welcome to Las Vegas sign.

title: Pecos has a second ceremonial name
body: Between Charleston and Owens, Pecos is also ceremonially signed Camino Cesar Chavez. The green Pecos signs and brown honorary signs share the same roadway; addresses did not change.

title: Nellis was a local fighter pilot
body: The boulevard points toward Nellis Air Force Base, renamed in 1950 for William Harrell Nellis. The Las Vegas High graduate was killed on his 70th combat mission during the Battle of the Bulge.

title: A planetarium on Cheyenne
body: The College of Southern Nevada's North Las Vegas campus sits at Cheyenne and Pecos. Its public planetarium and observatory make the campus an easy east-valley anchor.

title: This one really does reach Lake Mead
body: East Lake Mead Boulevard becomes State Route 147 at Pecos and continues to the Lake Mead National Recreation Area boundary. It is the north-valley route toward the lake.

title: Another road named as advertising
body: Flamingo Road takes its name from the resort where it crosses Las Vegas Boulevard. Several Strip cross streets were renamed for casinos in the late 1950s and early 1960s.

title: Roy Rogers had a ranch here
body: Cowboy star Roy Rogers bought Warm Springs Ranch after visiting Las Vegas for Helldorado. The ranch and its spring were southwest of today's Paradise and Warm Springs intersection.

title: Its old name was Salida del Sol
body: Both the north and south sections of Green Valley Parkway were formerly called Salida del Sol Drive. Henderson's street index records the older name across the corridor.

title: A state highway between two freeways
body: St. Rose Parkway is also State Route 146. Its state-maintained section links Interstate 15 with the 215 Beltway before continuing east through Henderson.

title: The west-side roads hid an alphabet
body: A former valley civil engineer traced an old west-side alphabet: Antelope, Buffalo, Cimarron, Durango, El Capitan, Fort Apache, Grand Canyon, then Hualapai. Summerlin's curved streets ended the sequence.

title: Named for a John Wayne western
body: A Clark County planning employee named Fort Apache Road for the 1948 John Ford western starring John Wayne and Henry Fonda. The same employee also named nearby streets after cars and television characters.

title: Not the Lamb most locals expect
body: Lamb Boulevard was named for Press Lamb—not Floyd Lamb, the former state senator behind the park's name. The prominent local family also included Sheriff Ralph Lamb and County Commissioner Darwin Lamb.

title: Lois Craig owned more than the ranch
body: Craig Road was named for Lois Craig, a North Las Vegas landowner. Nearby Craig Ranch began as farmland, became a golf course in the early 1960s, and later became a regional park.

title: The curve through Tivoli Village
body: Rampart bends past Boca Park and Tivoli Village at Alta Drive. Tivoli opened in 2011 with European-style architecture, turning the curve into one of the west valley's most recognizable retail corners.

title: A fly-fishing mural for a parks pioneer
body: Dundee Jones Park near Horizon Ridge and Jeffreys honors Henderson's former parks director. Its 33-foot fly-fishing mural celebrates Jones, who helped lay the foundation for the city's park system.

title: Where Las Vegas began with water
body: The Springs Preserve covers 180 acres along Valley View at the site of the springs that gave Las Vegas its name. Long before casinos, that water drew plants, animals, and people to the valley.

title: The city's oldest surviving schoolhouse
body: The Historic Westside School at Washington and D opened in 1923 as Las Vegas Grammar School Branch No. 1. It is the oldest surviving schoolhouse in Las Vegas.

title: The casino vanished; its road kept the name
body: Desert Inn Road was named for Wilbur Clark's resort. Clark also named Toni Avenue for his wife, Shirley Street for his father, LuLu Avenue for his mother, and Wilbur Street for himself.

title: The Welcome sign is just south
body: Betty Willis designed the Welcome to Fabulous Las Vegas sign in 1959. It stands in the Las Vegas Boulevard median just south of Russell Road, marking the Strip's familiar southern gateway.

title: From horse country to commuter route
body: East of Las Vegas Boulevard, Windmill Lane once crossed a quiet horse community. Development turned the former back road into a through route for commuters moving around the south valley.

title: Enough dirt for 1,800 swimming pools
body: A 2017 widening project on Blue Diamond Road moved enough dirt to fill 1,800 average swimming pools and used enough asphalt for 8,000 driveways. The road is also State Route 160.
